name: Lithuania
contextBlurb: As of 2025, Lithuania applies the EU MiCA regime: stablecoins are permitted as e-money tokens (EMTs) or asset-referenced tokens (ARTs), supervised by the Bank of Lithuania (MiCA NCA) with AML/CFT oversight by FCIS/FNTT. The Bank has issued Lithuania’s first CASP licence (Robinhood Europe UAB) and lists Ambr Payments UAB as an EMT issuer. From 2 Mar 2026, EMT activities that amount to payment services must be carried out by—or via—an appropriately licensed payment/e-money institution. EMTs carry par-value redemption; ARTs follow MiCA reserve, disclosure, and redemption rules.

legislation.title: Apr 2026 ESMA confirmed that the MiCA transitional period ends mid-2026; crypto firms must hold a full MiCA/CASP licence by that deadline. As of April 2026, 199 CASPs are licensed across the EU; no Asset-Referenced Token (ART) issuer has yet been approved.
FNTT publishes a BoL warning: only MiCA-licensed CASPs may operate in Lithuania/EU; firms must inform clients about licensing plans.
Bank of Lithuania issues the first CASP licence to Robinhood Europe UAB (passportable across the EU).
Seimas adopts Law XV-185 amending KRĮ Art. 22, extending the national VASP-registry transition until 1 Jan 2026.
Bank of Lithuania reminds that MiCA’s ART/EMT provisions have applied since 30 Jun 2024; CASPs must not offer non-compliant stablecoins.

stablecoinMeta.summaryEn: As of 2025, Lithuania applies the EU MiCA regime: stablecoins are permitted as e-money tokens (EMTs) or asset-referenced tokens (ARTs), supervised by the Bank of Lithuania (MiCA NCA) with AML/CFT oversight by FCIS/FNTT. The Bank has issued Lithuania’s first CASP licence (Robinhood Europe UAB) and lists Ambr Payments UAB as an EMT issuer. From 2 Mar 2026, EMT activities that amount to payment services must be carried out by—or via—an appropriately licensed payment/e-money institution. EMTs carry par-value redemption; ARTs follow MiCA reserve, disclosure, and redemption rules.
stablecoinMeta.classificationNote: In Lithuania, stablecoins fall under the EU MiCA framework as e-money tokens (EMTs) when they reference a single official currency and asset-referenced tokens (ARTs) when they reference multiple assets; they are not legal tender.
